perturbation.original_text: The term “ Person ” refers to any natural person, corporation, limited liability company, partnership, trust or other legal entity.
perturbation.changed_text: The term “ Person ” refers to any natural person or sole proprietorship.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition includes various legal entities like corporations and LLCs, which is standard. By narrowing the definition to only natural persons and sole proprietorships, we exclude many entities that are legally considered 'persons,' creating ambiguity and potential unenforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: 1 U.S.C. § 1 states that 'In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress, unless the context indicates otherwise... the words 'person' and 'whoever' include corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals.'  The modified definition directly contradicts this by excluding most of these entities from the definition of "Person."

perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 1 U.S. Code § 1 - Words denoting number, gender, and so forth | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 1 U.S. Code § 1 - Words denoting number, gender, and so forth U.S. Code Notes prev | next In determining the meaning of any Act of Congress , unless the context indicates otherwise— words importing the singular include and apply to several persons , parties, or things; words importing the plural include the singular; words importing the masculine gender include the feminine as well; words used in the present tense include the future as well as the present; the words “ insane ” and “ insane person ” shall include every idiot, insane person , and person non compos mentis; the words “ person ” and “whoever” include corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals ; “ officer ” includes any person authorized by law to perform the duties of the office; “ signature ” or “ subscription ” includes a mark when the person making the same intended it as such; “ oath ” includes affirmation, and “ sworn ” includes affirmed; “ writing ” includes printing and typewriting and reproductions of visual symbols by photographing, multigraphing, mimeographing, manifolding, or otherwise. (July 30, 1947, ch. 388, 61 Stat. 633 ; June 25, 1948, ch. 645, § 6, 62 Stat. 859 ; Oct. 31, 1951, ch. 655, § 1, 65 Stat. 710 ; Pub. L. 112–231, § 2(a) , Dec. 28, 2012 , 126 Stat. 1619 .) Editorial Notes Amendments 2012— Pub. L. 112–231 , in fifth clause after opening clause, struck out “and ‘lunatic’ ” before “shall include every” and “lunatic,” before “insane person, ”. 1951—Act Oct. 31, 1951 , substituted, in fourth clause after opening clause, “used” for “use”. 1948—Act June 25, 1948 , included “tense”, “whoever”, “signature” , “subscription” , “writing” and a broader definition of “person” . Statutory Notes and Related Subs
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts 1 U.S.C. § 1. The original text's definition of "Person" included various legal entities, aligning with the law. The changed text narrows the definition to only natural persons and sole proprietorships, explicitly excluding corporations, LLCs, partnerships, etc. The law snippet 2, states that "the words 'person' and 'whoever' include corporations, companies, associations, firms, partnerships, societies, and joint stock companies, as well as individuals." This exclusion directly contradicts the statute, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: The Recipient agrees to not engage, directly or indirectly, in transactions in Company securities while in possession of Confidential 2 Information, and further agrees that is will be responsible for its compliance with federal and state securities laws.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient agrees to only engage in transactions in Company securities with the express written consent of the Company's CEO while in possession of Confidential 2 Information, and further agrees that is will be responsible for its compliance with federal and state securities laws.
perturbation.explanation: This modification creates a scenario where insider trading laws could be violated despite having the CEO's permission, making the agreement contradictory and potentially unenforceable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 78j - Manipulative and deceptive devices
perturbation.law_explanation: 15 U.S.C. § 78j prohibits the use of manipulative or deceptive devices in connection with the purchase or sale of securities. Even with the CEO's permission, trading on non-public information would likely violate Rule 10b-5, as the CEO's permission doesn't negate the fact that the recipient is still trading on inside information. It does not matter if there is written consent, as such an action is a circumvention of security protocols.

Manipulative and deceptive devices It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce or of the mails, or of any facility of any national securities exchange- (a)(1) To effect a short sale, or to use or employ any stop-loss order in connection with the purchase or sale, of any security other than a government security, in contravention of such rules and regulatio
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a direct contradiction to securities laws. By stating that transactions are permissible with the CEO's written consent, it implies that insider trading is acceptable if authorized. However, 15 U.S.C. § 78j, as referenced in Scraped Law Snippet 1, prohibits manipulative or deceptive devices in securities trading, regardless of internal permissions. The Law Explanation supports this by stating CEO permission doesn't negate trading on inside information, which circumvents security protocols. The modification undermines the core intent of insider trading regulations, establishing a scenario where illegal activity is seemingly condoned.

perturbation.original_text: The Parties will pay their own expenses with respect to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: The Recipient will pay all expenses with respect to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change could violate state laws regarding the enforceability of contracts where one party bears all the costs, especially if the agreement disproportionately benefits one party. This shifts all financial burden to one party, potentially voiding the contract.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Delaware Code Title 6, § 2704. Lack of consideration as defense
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law requires consideration for a contract to be enforceable. While this change doesn't explicitly eliminate consideration, it places the entire financial burden on the Recipient, making it look like an unconscionable contract where one party receives all the benefit, potentially negating the initial consideration. This could lead a court to determine there was inadequate consideration, thus making the contract unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The modification places the entire financial burden on the 'Recipient,' diverging from the original agreement where 'Parties will pay their own expenses.' This shift creates a potential issue of unconscionability, where one party bears a disproportionate burden. The Law Explanation highlights that Delaware law requires consideration for a contract to be enforceable, and such a shift could be viewed as undermining the initial consideration. Although the provided snippet from the Delaware Code does not directly address consideration, the Law Explanation suggests this shift could make the contract unenforceable due to a lack of fair exchange, thereby contradicting the principle of valid consideration implied within contract law.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware applicable to agreements made and to be performed within such state, without giving effect to any choice of law principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the People's Republic of China applicable to agreements made and to be performed within such state, without giving effect to any choice of law principles.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to that of a foreign country may not be enforceable under US law, especially if the agreement involves US-based entities and assets, contradicting established legal principles of jurisdiction and enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 deals with choice of law in contract disputes. While parties are generally free to choose the governing law, this choice is not absolute. A US court may refuse to enforce a choice of law provision if it violates a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state. Selecting PRC law for an agreement between two US companies operating primarily in the US would likely be considered against public policy and disregarded, especially if the PRC law provides less protection for intellectual property or allows actions (like breach of confidentiality) that are illegal under US law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-301. Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power to Choose Applicable Law. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-301. Territorial Applicability; Parties' Power to Choose Applicable Law. a) Except as otherwise provided in this section, when a transaction bears a reasonable relation to this state and also to another state or nation the parties may agree that the law either of this state or of such other state or nation shall govern their rights and duties. (b) In the absence of an agreement effective under subsection (a), and except as provided in subsection (c), [the Uniform Commercial Code] applies to transactions bearing an appropriate relation to this state. (c) If one of the following provisions of [the Uniform Commercial Code] specifies the applicable law, that provision governs and a contrary agreement is effective only to the extent permitted by the law so specified: (1) Section 2-402 ; (2) Sections 2A-105 and 2A-106 ; (3) Section 4-102 ; (4) Section 4A-507 ; (5) Section 5-116 ; [(6) Section 6-103 ; (7) Section 8-110 ; (8) Sections 9-301 through 9 - 307 . perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts established legal principles by changing the governing law from Delaware to the People's Republic of China. This introduces a conflict because, as the Law Explanation states, a US court may refuse to enforce a choice of law provision if it violates a fundamental policy of a state which has a materially greater interest than the chosen state. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that parties may agree to the law of another state or nation shall govern their rights and duties, provided the transaction bears a reasonable relation to that state or nation. Here, the Perturbation Explanation specifies that the agreement involves US-based entities and assets, which would likely make the choice of PRC law unenforceable.
